slides.title: Type Variables 🧬
slides.content:
Just like standard variables (`let x = 5`) store values, Generics act as variables that store **Types**.

We declare a Generic type variable using angle brackets `<Type>`. By deep convention, developers use a single capital `<T>` (short for Type).

slides.title: The Generic Implementation
slides.content: We declare `<T>` right before the function parameters. Then, we can use `T` as our parameter type AND our return type!

quizData.question: What is the primary immense benefit achieved by using `<T>` here instead of `any`?

options.text: It executes 10x faster natively in JS.
TypeScript dynamically locks the return type. We passed a string, so TS guarantees with 100% certainty that `output` is deeply recognized as a string.
It securely encrypts the string data.

quizData.explanation: Generics preserve the type identity! It perfectly mirrors the type in and out, preventing it from devolving into 'any'.

slides.title: Magical Type Inference 🎩
slides.content:
In practice, you rarely need to explicitly write `<string>` when calling the function.

TypeScript compiler is overwhelmingly smart; it looks at the argument `"Hello"` and perfectly infers that `T` must be a string automatically!

slides.title: Readability 👀
slides.content: Whenever you see `<T>` inside a function, read it internally as: "For some unknown Type T..."
